coredata.dc:title: Rapid microwave-assisted green synthesis of guanine-derived carbon dots for highly selective detection of Ag+ in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Quan
Bai, Zhile
Xi, Xingjun
Guo, Zhiwei
Liu, Cong
Liu, Xuerui
Zhao, Xiaoyan
Li, Zhiyue
Cheng, Yong
Wei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple and green synthetic approach of novel guanine decorated carbon dots (G-CDs) using guanosine 5′-monophosphate and ethylenediamine through a domestic microwave oven was established for the first time. The as-prepared fluorescent G-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The obtained fluorescent G-CDs with a uniform morphology had desirable functional groups and excellent optical performances. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was remarkably quenched by Ag+ than that of other nucleotides-derived CDs. The density functional theory calculations were performed to confirm that the strong interaction of guanine-Ag+ was responsible for the remarkable fluorescence response of G-CDs towards Ag+. In addition, as a label-free fluorescence probe, the G-CDs displayed a good linear detection for highly selective Ag+ sensing over the range of 0–80 μM with the low detection limit of 90 nM. Therefore, the proposed G-CDs had the capacity for Ag+ detection in the real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave synthesis
Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Ag+ detection
Mechanism analysis

Analysis of Ag+ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Sinopec Ministry of Science and Technology Basic Prospective Research Project

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the G-CDs. (b) The particle size distribution of the G-CDs. (c) The XPS spectra of the G-CDs. (d) The FT-IR spectra of the G-CDs.

(a) Absorption (black line), fluorescence excitation (red line), and fluorescence emission (green line) spectra of the G-CDs in aqueous solution. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the G-CDs at different excitation wavelength.
(a) The FL intensity of the G-CDs at different pH values. (b) The FL intensity of the G-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of NaCl.

(a) The FL responses of the G-CDs to the addition of different metal ions (the concentration of Ag+ is 100 μM, the concentration of other metal ions is 200 μM). F0 and F correspond to the FL intensities of the G-CDs in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. The inset in (a) is the corresponding photograph of the G-CDs with different metal ions under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. (b) The FL spectra of the G-CDs upon addition of different concentration of Ag+ (from top to bottom: 0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100 μM, respectively). Inset of Fig. 4b is the picture of the G-CDs solutions before and after adding 100 μM Ag+ under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. (c) The relationship between the F0/F and Ag+ concentrations (0–80 μM).
The FL quenching effect (F0/F) of (a) A-CDs, (b) C-CDs, and (c) U-CDs towards 100 μM Ag+, respectively.
Optimized geometries of (a) guanine-Ag+, (b) adenine-Ag+, (c) cytosine-Ag+, (d) uracil-Ag+ complexes, respectively.
The Lineweaver-Burk fitting linearity for G-CDs and Ag+.
Schematic illustration of the microwave synthesis of the fluorescent G-CDs for Ag+ sensor.
Schematic illustration for the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of the G-CDs by Ag+.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection.

head.ce:title: Rapid microwave-assisted green synthesis of guanine-derived carbon dots for highly selective detection of Ag+ in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, 15 3rd North East Road, Chaoyang District, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, 15 3rd North East Road, Chaoyang District, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre on Translational Neuroscience, College of Life and Environmental Sciences, Minzu University of China, 27 Zhongguancun South Avenue, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre on Translational Neuroscience, College of Life and Environmental Sciences, Minzu University of China, 27 Zhongguancun South Avenue, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China National Institute of Standardization, Zhong guancun South Avenue, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China National Institute of Standardization, Zhong guancun South Avenue, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrafast and facile microwave-assisted synthesis of guanine-derived carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of Ag+.

In this work, a simple and green synthetic approach of novel guanine decorated carbon dots (G-CDs) using guanosine 5′-monophosphate and ethylenediamine through a domestic microwave oven was established for the first time. The as-prepared fluorescent G-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The obtained fluorescent G-CDs with a uniform morphology had desirable functional groups and excellent optical performances. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was remarkably quenched by Ag+ than that of other nucleotides-derived CDs. The density functional theory calculations were performed to confirm that the strong interaction of guanine-Ag+ was responsible for the remarkable fluorescence response of G-CDs towards Ag+. In addition, as a label-free fluorescence probe, the G-CDs displayed a good linear detection for highly selective Ag+ sensing over the range of 0–80 μM with the low detection limit of 90 nM. Therefore, the proposed G-CDs had the capacity for Ag+ detection in the real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, due to the widespread use of the heavy metal ions in factories, their consequent pollution has become a severe worldwide problem with their potential harm to the human body and the environment [1]. As one of the heavy metal, silver ion (Ag+) is of great significance to the development of photography, imaging, pharmacy, and electrical industry with its attractive properties [2,3]. It is believed that Ag+ can combine with various metabolites and enzymes, which can cause the enzymes inactivation and result in health problems [4–6]. Previously reported analytical methods for Ag+ sensing include atomic absorption spectrometry [7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8], and stripping voltammetry method [9,10]. However, many of them often involve specialized instrumentation, complicated sample pre-treatment, and time-consuming analysis, which severely limit their further development. Compared to these methods, the fluorescence analysis has been a promising alternative method due to its advantages of low cost, fast analysis, and good reproducibility. Up to now, various fluorescent materials have been established for the analysis of Ag+, including organic dyes [11], semiconductor quantum dots [12], and noble-metal clusters [13]. Nevertheless, the above fluorescent probes still suffer from some drawbacks with their potential toxicity, high cost, low sensitivity, and complex preparation.

As an emerging fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively investigated in biosensing [14], bioimaging [15], drug delivery [16], catalysis [17], and light emitting diode materials [18] owing to their high aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and good photostability. Currently, various fluorescent CDs have been designed for metal ions detection with their excellent optical and chemical properties. For example, Chen's group reported a new electrochemical strategy for the synthesis of GQDs by chemical vapour deposition for sensitive and specific sensor to Fe3+ [19]. Wang and co-workers described a simple fluorescent sensing method for the detection of Cu2+ based on luminescent GQDs prepared by chemical oxidation approach from 3D nanomesh graphene frameworks [20]. Zhuo et al. proposed a turn-off fluorescent detection method for Hg2+ using the N,S-CDs synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal method from [C4mim][Cys] ionic liquid [21]. However, these approaches usually need expensive equipment, toxic or expensive raw materials, long-time consumption, and harsh synthetic conditions, which limit the development and application of CDs. Consequently, the microwave-assisted synthesis has been considered as a green, efficient, and time-saving alternative method for the preparation of CDs [22–24]. In addition, to the best of our knowledge, the reported fluorescent analysis method for Ag+ detection based on CDs is still rare [25,26]. Moreover, the selectivity of some CDs for the detection of Ag+ is unsatisfactory because the interference metal ions including Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ can also quench the fluorescence of the CDs by the easy electron transfer process with CDs when they coexist in the CDs solutions [27–29]. From the perspective of synthesis process and sensing application, developing a new strategy toward simple and green synthesis of CDs for highly selective and sensitive towards Ag+ sensing is still highly desired.

Nucleotides, as essential biomolecules of RNA and DNA, are versatile for metal coordination with their multiplex binding sites. In this contribution, several recent researchers have designed ingenious nucleotide-directed biosensors for metal detection, such as nucleotide-hybrid lanthanide coordination polymer and nucleotide-stabilized gold nanoclusters [30–32]. However, these above nanomaterials still have some hampers with complex operation or using expensive reagent. In this work, an ultrafast and green synthetic method for the preparation of novel guanine-derived CDs (G-CDs) with the precursor of guanosine 5′-monophosphate (GMP) and the cross-linking agent of ethylenediamine (EDA) by using home-use microwave oven was established for the first time. The resulting fluorescent G-CDs had remarkable water solubility and exhibited strongly pH and salt stability. Importantly, as a fluorescent probe, the obtained G-CDs can be used for the highly sensitive detection of Ag+ despite the presence of other metal ions owing to the specific interaction between Ag+ and guanine. Meanwhile, the detection of Ag+ in real water samples using the G-CDs was also successfully achieved.

Guanosine 5′-monophosphate disodium salt and quinine sulfate were purchased from Macklin Company. Adenosine 5′-monophosphate sodium salt (AMP), cytidine 5′-monophosphate disodium salt (CMP), disodium uridine-5′-monophosphate (UMP), and ethylenediamine was purchased from Aladdin Company. All the chemical regents used in the experiment were of analytical grade.

Transition electronic microscopy (TEM) image of the G-CDs was acquired through a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope operated at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). The Escalab 250 (Thermo Fisher, USA) photoelectron spectrometer was operated to get the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was obtained through a Nicolet 8700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) using the KBr method. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were examined on a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of the G-CDs were recorded using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The Perkin Elmer-8000 inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry ICP-OES (PerkinElmer, USA) was used for Ag+ concentration detection.

Typically, 1 mmol of GMP and 300 μL of EDA were dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the mixed solution in a 50 mL glass beaker was placed in a household microwave oven (700 W, Midea, China) and heated for 10 min. After the reaction, the yellow solid product was re-dissolved in 10 mL of water and then purified with a dialysis bag (MWCO = 500). Finally, the obtained G-CDs solution in the bag was stored in a refrigerator for the further application.

The quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized G-CDs was measured with quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference (QY = 54%) according to the reported method [33,34]. The QY of the G-CDs was determined by using the following equation:
Y x
=
Y s
×
A s
A x
×
F x
F s
×
n
x
2
n
r
2
where Y stands for the QY, F represents the integrated emission intensity, n denotes the refraction index, and A is the optical density. The subscript s refers to the standard (quinine sulfate) and x refers to the G-CDs.

In a typical assay, the G-CDs (100 μL) solution was mixed with 800 μL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.0). Then, 100 μL Ag+ solutions with different concentrations were added and incubated for about 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with the excitation at 365 nm. The selectivity of the G-CDs was examined by adding the interfered metal ions such as Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Mg2+.

The toxicity of the G-CDs was measured by the water-soluble tetrazolium (WST) assay. PANC (pancreatic cancer) cells were seeded into 96 well plates with the density of 3 × 103 cells per well, and incubated in an incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, different concentrations of the G-CDs were introduced to each well and cultured in a 37 °C incubator for another 24 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of premixed WST-1 was added into each well and cultured at 37 °C for 2 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured with a multi-well plate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 , the fluorescent G-CDs were synthesized via the simple microwave-assistant treatment of GMP and EDA. Due to the use of domestic microwave oven and without any other toxic agents, one-pot microwave heating method makes the entire experiment process rapid, low cost, and environmentally friendly.

Although the mechanism of carbon dots formation is not clear, we proposed a reasonable hypothesis of the synthetic process of carbon dots. Firstly, GMP was quickly conjunct with EDA through the chemical reaction under the microwave heating. Then, these cross-linked polymers were generated by continuous blending and condensation. During the further heating, these polymers were subsequently carbonized to form carbon dots. Because the different reaction conditions for CDs formation could affect their corresponding fluorescence (FL) efficiency, the molar ratio of precursors and the reaction timewere studied in this work. As shown in Fig. S1a, when the addition of GMP was fixed with 1 mmol, the FL intensity of the G-CDs significantly increased by introducing EDA into the precursor solution, and achieved the maximum value at 300 μL of EDA, which revealing that the addition of the cross-linking agent EDA into the synthetic system is crucial role to enhance the FL efficiency of CDs. Moreover, the optimal reaction time was 10 min to obtain the highest FL intensity of the G-CDs in the presence of 300 μL EDA (Fig. S1b). Consequently, the optimum conditions were fixed as follows: 1 mmol GMP, 300 μL EDA, and 10 min reaction time.

As shown in TEM image (Fig. 1 a) and HR-TEM (Fig. S2a), the G-CDs showed a spherical morphology and little graphitic lattices, which indicated the G-CDs was amorphous. The average size of G-CDs was about 3.75 nm from the size distribution diagram (Fig. 1b). The XRD diffractogram of the G-CDs (Fig. S2b) showed the typical peaks at around 20.6° and 27.5°, which were assigned to highly disordered carbon atoms. And the zeta potential of the G-CDs with negative charge density was –22.9 mV, which made the particles well dispersed in the aqueous solution (Fig. S3). The XPS and FT-IR experiments were performed to reveal the chemical compositions and surface structures of the as-prepared G-CDs. As seen from the XPS spectrum of the G-CDs in Fig. 1c, the four strong binding energy peaks at 284.8, 399.0, 532.2, and 132.6 eV were ascribed to C1s, N1s, O1s, and P2p with an atomic concentration of 58.6%, 14.6%, 24.0%, and 2.8%, respectively. The binding energy at 284.8, 286.1, and 287.7 eV in the C1s spectrum (Fig. S4a) were ascribed to C=C, C-N/C-O, and C=O groups, respectively. As displayed in Fig. S4b, the XPS spectrum of N1s indicated the presence of three different peaks at 398.9, 399.9, and 400.4 eV, which were attributed to –C=N-H, N-H, and C-N groups, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. S4c, the binding energy at around 530.8 and 532.3 eV in the O1s spectrum were associated with the C=O and C-OH/C-O-C bands, respectively. The P-O (132.5 eV) and P-C/P-N (133.2 eV) were observed in the high-resolution spectrum of P2p (Fig. S4d). The broad absorption bands at around 3426 and 3117 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 d) related to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H groups, respectively. The 1694, 1536, and 1481 cm−1 absorption bands were assigned to the stretching vibration of C=O, N–H, and C–N, respectively. Moreover, the typical stretching vibration of P=O band was observed at 1084 cm−1. The FT-IR results were in consistent well with the XPS data, which indicated that the surface of G-CDs had versatile functional groups.

Fig. 2a presented the optical properties of the G-CDs, the typical absorption peak at 350 nm was attributed to the n-π* electronic transition of the C=O and C=N bonds, resulting in the fluorescence emission due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states [35]. It was found that the maximum FL emission of the G-CDs was located at 465 nm when excited at 365 nm. Fig. 2b showed the emission wavelength of the G-CDs was red-shifted under the excitation wavelength changing from 320 nm to 420 nm. As generally observed in most of CDs, the excitation-dependent FL phenomenon was related to the diverse emissive trap sites and different sizes of the G-CDs [36,37]. When utilizing the G-CDs for fluorescence detection, the material should be stable toward ambient environments. As seen from in Fig. 3 a, it should be noticed that the FL intensity of the G-CDs was very stable under various pH ranging from 3 to 11. Fig. 3b showed the FL intensity remained almost constant even under 500 mM NaCl, which showed the G-CDs had excellent salt tolerance. Furthermore, the QY of the G-CDs was estimated as 10.4% using quinine sulfate as the reference. These results suggested the obtained G-CDs could be potentially used for sensing under physiological conditions.

Taking the significant advantages above, the resulting G-CDs had the potential to be a novel fluorescent probe. Interestingly, it was observed that the FL intensity of the G-CDs was obviously quenched with Ag+, indicating the present FL probe could be used for Ag+ detection. To evaluate the selectivity of the G-CDs for Ag+ detection, the FL intensity of G-CDs with 100 μM of Ag+ and various interferential metal ions (200 μM) including Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Mg2+ were measured under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 a, only Ag+ could induce a prominent FL quenching towards the G-CDs in contrast to the other interferential metal ions. The corresponding photograph of G-CDs solutions with different metal ions under UV lamp (insets in Fig. 4a) were in agreement with the results of FL spectra. Such results directly indicated the G-CDs had good selectivity for Ag+ response.

The sensitivity for Ag+ measurement based on the as-synthesized G-CDs was carried out by the addition of different concentrations of Ag+ (0–100 μM) into the G-CDs solutions. Fig. 4b showed that the FL intensity of the G-CDs decreased gradually with increasing Ag+ concentration. In addition, the FL quenching effect of the G-CDs with 100 μM Ag+ was easily observed from the photograph under the UV lamp (inset of Fig. 4b). The FL quenching constant of G-CDs could be evaluated by using the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
where
K
SV
refers to the Stern-Volmer constant,
F 0
and
F
are the FL intensity at 465 nm in the absence and presence of Ag+, respectively,
Q
is the concentration of Ag+ [38,39]. As shown in Fig. 4c, with the concertation of Ag+ ranging from 0 to 80 μM, the calibration curve of F0/F = 0.034CAg + + 0.959 with a good linear correlation of R2 = 0.9966 was obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) of Ag+ was 0.09 μM (S/N = 3), which is considerably much lower than the maximum allowable level (0.93 μM) for the Ag+ ions in drinking water as regulated by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [40]. Moreover, as listed in Table 1 , the fluorescent sensing methodology based on the obtained G-CDs for Ag+ sensing had much simpler operation, better selectivity, and lower detection compared with those given in the literatures.

It is evident that the nucleotides have the excellent metal coordination ability with the inherent groups of base and phosphate. Obviously, the surface of G-CDs made from GMP were endowed with multiple recognition sites for metal ions, which were beneficial for Ag+ detection. However, it was still ambiguous to us which group on the surface of the G-CDs played a decisive role for Ag+ sensing. A series of researches have shown that GMP has the strong coordination ability with Ag+ due to the unique binding link of guanine-Ag+ [41,42]. Thus, we speculated that the highly selectivity of the obtained G-CDs for Ag+ sensing was attributed to the particular recognition sites of guanine on the surface of G-CDs. In order to confirm the hypothesis, we also have synthesized other nucleotide-derived CDs including A-CDs, C-CDs, and U-CDs (produced from AMP, CMP, and UMP) under the same reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. S5, the A-CDs, C-CDs, and U-CDs showed the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of 395/461 nm, 380/466 nm, and 400/475 nm, respectively. Then, we further explored the FL response of the nucleotide-derived CDs treated with 100 μM Ag+. And the FL quenching effect (F0/F) of A-CDs, C-CDs, and U-CDs were 1.22, 1.42, and 1.37(Fig. 5 ), which were much lower than that of G-CDs with 3.75. Since these nucleotide-derived CDs were synthesized from nucleotide and EDA under the same condition, the surface of these nucleotide-derived CDs were equipped with the same groups including phosphate, hydroxyl, and amino, but only with different group of bases. The guanine on the surface of the G-CDs should be of crucial importance for the significant FL response to Ag+. To further understand the strength of the interactions of base and Ag+, the binding capacity of the bases to Ag+ were computed by the density functional theory (DFT) calculations using Gaussian 09 (detailed calculating parameters shown in supporting information). The optimized geometries of the mentioned base-Ag+ complexes models were shown in Fig. 6 . And the binding energies of guanine-Ag+, adenine-Ag+, cytosine-Ag+, uracil-Ag+ were calculated as −72.33 kcal/mol, −51.34 kcal/mol, −67.90 kcal/mol, and −44.46 kcal/mol, respectively. The highest binding energy of guanine-Ag+ indicated that Ag+ would prefer to strongly adsorb on G-CDs than on the other nucleotide-derived CDs, subsequently caused the significant FL quenching of G-CDs. Based on the above results, the G-CDs have the superior selectivity for Ag+ detection, which could be ascribed to the strong interaction of guanine and Ag+.

To further reveal the possible FL quenching mechanism of G-CDs by Ag+, the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and the FL lifetime experiment were carried out. As shown in Fig. S6a, the absorption band at 350 nm significantly increased with the addition of 100 μM Ag+, which was possible to the interaction between G-CDs and Ag+. And the binding constant (K) between the G-CDs and Ag+ can be calculated using the following Lineweaver-Burk equation [43]:
1
F 0
- F
=
1
F 0
+
1
KF
0
Q

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Ag+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the superior specificity and high sensitivity of the response of the G-CDs to Ag+, this fluorescent probe could be used for Ag+ detection in real samples. The WST assays for the PANCs cell were carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the G-CDs prior to detection in the real samples. As shown from the cytotoxicity test in Fig. S9, the obtained G-CDs were low toxic and excellently biocompatible, which suggested the G-CDs could be used in the environmental and biological detection without any pollution and side effects. The determination ability of Ag+ with the real water samples of tap water and river water were further investigated. Both samples were filtered by a 0.22-μm filtering membrane before detection. Table S1 showed that the recoveries of Ag+ sensing for each sample were in the range of 95.8–105.9%. In addition, the relative standard deviations (RSD) for the detection of Ag+ in each sample were below 5%. In order to further investigate the feasibility and accuracy of the G-CDs for Ag+ detection in serum. The concentrations of Ag+ in spiked blood serum samples were determined by using G-CDs sensors, and validated using ICP-OES method. As shown in Table S2, the obtained results by using the prepared G-CDs method were consistent with that obtained via ICP-OES method. Based on the above results, the developed G-CDs sensor might have great potential for the real sample analysis.

In conclusion, a rapid, economical, and environment-friendly microwave-assisted method for the preparation of the G-CDs using a domestic microwave was proposed. The as-synthesized G-CDs was excellently water-soluble and remarkable stable against different pH and extreme ionic strengths. Furthermore, a label-free fluorescent probe based on the resulting G-CDs for excellently selective analysis of Ag+ with an LOD of 90 nM was obtained. Finally, the present fluorescent probe was successfully utilized in the real samples for Ag+ detection. We believed that this strategy might provide new ideas for the biological and environmental applications with simple operation, low cytotoxicity, and excellent optical performance.

Quan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Zhile Bai: Formal analysis, Investigation. Xingjun Xi: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Zhiwei Guo: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Cong Liu: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Xuerui Liu: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Xiaoyan Zhao: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Zhiyue Li: Writing - review & editing. Yong Cheng: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yun Wei: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the SINOPEC Research and Development Program (Grant No. 217015-6 & 420008-1), Special Fundamental Research Fund for the Central Public Scientific Research Institutes (Grant No. 562019Y-6671) and the (XK1802-6).Thanks Miss xiaoyan Zhao for her providing the blood serum for this experiment.
This article containing the study with human participant performed was approved by the ethical committee of Beijing University of Chemical Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119208.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical soil concentrations of cadmium, lead, and mercury in view of health effects on humans and animals

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of precious metal ions (silver, gold and platinum ions)

Sun, Study on solid phase extraction and graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry for the determination of nickel, silver, cobalt, copper, cadmium and lead with MCI GEL CHP 20Y as sorbent, J.
